tldr: Headlines across 10 countries deliver an almost uniform arrest-crime frame: China has detained US scholar Min Zin on espionage charges. Only The Independent adds a diplomatic dimension, linking the arrest to Trump's recent visit to Beijing.

frames.sub_frame: China arrests US scholar for espionage

evidence.quote: China arrests US researcher it says is suspected of 'spying'
China says US national arrested on suspected espionage
中国、米国人のミャンマー研究者を拘束
China arrests US scholar of Myanmar on suspicion of spying
China detains US citizen suspected of spying
China confirms arrest of US citizen suspected of spying
China arrests US scholar of Myanmar on suspicion of spying
Beijing says US analyst detained for alleged espionage
on espionage charges just weeks after Trump trip

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to contextualise the arrest against US-China diplomatic relations, linking it temporally to Trump's recent Beijing visit — framing the espionage charge as a diplomatic signal rather than a standalone law-enforcement event.
Only outlet to specify Min Zin was arrested 'at conference', adding the situational detail of his presence at a meeting — absent from all other headlines.

Second Asahi article in this cluster (index 4) covers an unrelated story — ex-South Korean president Yoon's 30-year sentence and North Korea drone incidents — suggesting a cluster ingestion anomaly in the japan bucket.

bottom_line: Headline coverage presents the Min Zin detention as a straightforward espionage arrest with near-total framing consensus across continents; the sole outlier is The Independent's implicit suggestion that the arrest's timing vis-à-vis Trump's Beijing visit makes it a diplomatic act as much as a criminal one.
